coredata.dc:title: Development and characterization of hydrogel beads with carboxymethyl chitosan/graphene quantum dots@Pectin/MIL-88 for targeted doxorubicin delivery: An adaptable nanocomposite approach
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Gholamali, Iman
Yadollahi, Mehdi
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogels are adaptable substances with a 3D framework able to hold large quantities of water, which is why they are ideal for use in the field of biomedicine. This research project focused on creating a new hydrogel combining carboxymethyl chitosan (CMCS), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), pectin (Pe), and MIL-88 for precise and controlled release of the cancer drug doxorubicin (DOX). The creation of CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 hydrogel beads was achieved through an eco-friendly one-step synthesis method. The hydrogel beads were then analyzed using various techniques including FE-SEM, EDX, FT-IR, XRD, BET surface area, DLS, and zeta potential measurements. The hydrogel beads showed great swelling ability and controlled breakdown in different pH environments, mimicking the conditions of the gastrointestinal tract and body. Research on drug loading and release showed that the hydrogel components can be adjusted to control the release of DOX. Cytotoxicity tests in a lab setting using K562 cells demonstrated successful delivery of DOX and the ability to target cancer cells specifically while reducing negative effects. Adding GQDs improved both the imaging abilities and the stability and mechanical characteristics of the hydrogel. This research indicates that the CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 combination hydrogel beads show great potential for advanced drug delivery systems, especially in cancer treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carboxymethyl chitosan
Graphene quantum dots
Pectin
MIL-88
Hydrogel beads
Doxorubicin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In-situ synthesis of CMCS/GQDs within the Pe/MIL-88 hydrogel beads (CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88)
Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX)

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra for the DOX (a), MIL-88 (b), pure Pe (c), pure CMCS (d), CMCS/Pe hydrogel, CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel and CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88/DOX hydrogel.
X-ray diffraction patterns of pure CMCS (a), pure Pe (b), MIL-88 (c), CMCS/Pe hydrogel (d) and CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel.
Zeta potential of the GQD and CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 (a) and DLS curve of CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 (b).
The FE-SEM images of MIL-88 (a), CMCS/Pe hydrogel (b) and CMCS/GQD@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel (c), the EDX spectrum of CMCS/GQD@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel beads (d).

Swelling behavior of CMCS/Pe hydrogel beads and CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel beads in simulated gastrointestinal tract passage (pH and time) (a), Biodegradation behavior of the CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite beads in PBS (pH 7.4) at 37 °C for different time (b).
The standard calibration curve of DOX (a). Comparison of the in vitro DOX release pattern from CMCS/Pe hydrogel and CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel at simulated GIT condition (b).
In-vitro cell viabilities of K562 cells after being incubated for 72h with various concentrations of free DOX, CMCS/Pe/DOX hydrogel beads and CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88/DOX nanocomposite hydrogel beads.
Schematic presentation of CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel beads.

head.ce:title: Development and characterization of hydrogel beads with carboxymethyl chitosan/graphene quantum dots@Pectin/MIL-88 for targeted doxorubicin delivery: An adaptable nanocomposite approach

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nanopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nanopolymers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, North Tehran Branch, Islamic Azad University, P.O. Box 19585/936, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, North Tehran Branch, Islamic Azad University, P.O. Box 19585/936, Tehran, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nanopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nanopolymers

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogels are adaptable substances with a 3D framework able to hold large quantities of water, which is why they are ideal for use in the field of biomedicine. This research project focused on creating a new hydrogel combining carboxymethyl chitosan (CMCS), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), pectin (Pe), and MIL-88 for precise and controlled release of the cancer drug doxorubicin (DOX). The creation of CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 hydrogel beads was achieved through an eco-friendly one-step synthesis method. The hydrogel beads were then analyzed using various techniques including FE-SEM, EDX, FT-IR, XRD, BET surface area, DLS, and zeta potential measurements. The hydrogel beads showed great swelling ability and controlled breakdown in different pH environments, mimicking the conditions of the gastrointestinal tract and body. Research on drug loading and release showed that the hydrogel components can be adjusted to control the release of DOX. Cytotoxicity tests in a lab setting using K562 cells demonstrated successful delivery of DOX and the ability to target cancer cells specifically while reducing negative effects. Adding GQDs improved both the imaging abilities and the stability and mechanical characteristics of the hydrogel. This research indicates that the CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 combination hydrogel beads show great potential for advanced drug delivery systems, especially in cancer treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data presented in this study are available upon request from the corresponding author.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogels are remarkable substances that consist of a network of polymer chains capable of holding a significant amount of water while maintaining their structure [1,2]. The polymer chains in hydrogels are crosslinked, either physically or chemically, giving them a three-dimensional structure. Hydrogels can derive from natural polymers like chitosan, alginate, and pectin that often used due to their biocompatibility [3,4]. Some common applications of hydrogels include personal care products [5], drug delivery [6], wound dressings [7], contact lenses [8] and tissue engineering scaffolds [9].

Carboxymethyl chitosan (CMCS) is a modified form of chitosan that contains carboxyl groups. This alteration process gives the material improved solubility in water compared to native chitosan. The enhanced solubility allows CMCS to be formed into hydrogels. These hydrogels have valuable properties for many application areas. Due to their high water absorption, CMCS hydrogels can maintain a moist environment ideal for wound healing. The gel structure also enables controlled release of drugs over time. This controlled release feature enhances the effectiveness of pharmaceutical compounds delivered through the hydrogel. Both the improved water solubility and ability for controlled drug delivery impart practical advantages over traditional biopolymers. As a result, CMCS hydrogels show promise for uses in medical products and therapies to benefit human health. Collagen-based proteins show promise for medical uses due to their cell-friendly and temporary nature [10]. Pectin (Pe) is a natural polysaccharide primarily found in the cell walls of plants, especially in fruits such as apples and citrus. Its popularity in the food industry as a thickening agent is well-known, but its properties of being biocompatible, biodegradable, and non-toxic have also attracted attention in the biomedical and pharmaceutical sectors [11]. When Pe is linked together to create a hydrogel, it has the ability to trap medications and slowly release them, making Pe hydrogels a useful substance for drug delivery systems. Pe hydrogels can encapsulate drugs and release them over time. The release rate can be controlled by modifying factors like the degree of cross-linking, the pectin concentration, and the encapsulated drug's properties [12,13]. Graphene quantum dot nanoparticles (GQDs) have recently captured considerable interest owing to their distinctive characteristics, including pronounced quantum confinement and notable edge effects [14,15]. Researchers have been vigorously investigating both bottom-up and top-down synthesis techniques to produce GQDs and to delve into their properties [16,17]. Nevertheless, their applicability in the biomedical sector remains largely uncharted. GQDs possess several remarkable characteristics including high intrinsic fluorescence, a large surface area with delocalized electrons, exceptional excitation-dependent emission, solubility in various solvents, chemical stability, and functional groups on their edges [18,19]. These features make GQDs highly suitable for biomedical applications, particularly in cellular imaging and drug delivery [20,21]. Their carbon-based composition ensures low toxicity, especially when compared to inorganic quantum dots that may release toxic metal ions upon degradation. The ability of GQDs to load drugs through π-π interactions and their versatile size and shape further enhance their potential as a drug delivery platform, underscoring their promise in advancing biomedical technologies [22,23]. The choice of the Fe-based MOF, MIL-88 (Fe) (Material Institute Lavoisier), was based on its superb design flexibility, low toxicity, and capability for controlled substance release [24]. MIL-88 frameworks consist of metal ions, such as iron, chromium, or aluminum, bonded to organic compounds like terephthalic acid [25,26].

Doxorubicin (DOX) is a powerful chemotherapy medication utilized for the treatment of different cancers such as breast cancer [27], leukemia [28], lymphoma [29], and sarcomas [30]. Nevertheless, the notable adverse effects call for the creation of specialized delivery systems to lessen overall toxicity within the body [31]. In this context, DOX is encapsulated within a nanoscale composite in order to more effectively deliver it directly to cancer cells, thereby lessening negative side effects and improving therapeutic outcomes. The composite utilizes MIL-88, which effectively encapsulates DOX due to its high porosity and large surface area. After administration, the composite preferentially accumulates at tumor sites thanks to the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect [25,32]. The acidic conditions within the tumor microenvironment then trigger the release of DOX from MIL-88, aided by the degradation of Pe and CMCS. What's more, GQDs within the composite provide imaging abilities, allowing for real-time monitoring of the distribution, biological imaging and release of the drug [33,34].

As part of our research into advanced drug delivery methods, we developed a novel biomaterial composite. This green single-step synthesis yielded a CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel beads suitable for use as a drug carrier. The anticancer drug DOX was loaded efficiently into the CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 system. Release experiments showed it was possible to control the rate of drug release by manipulating the ratios of the three component materials. Initial results indicate this carrier holds promise for targeted anticancer therapies. A multi-technique analytical approach provided comprehensive characterization of the resulting hydrogel. To thoroughly investigate the characteristics of the synthesized nanocomposite hydrogel beads, we utilized a range of analytical methods, including FE-SEM, EDX, FT-IR, XRD, BET surface area analysis, DLS and zeta potential measurements. This allowed selection of an ideal composite formulation with engineered properties geared for clinical efficacy. Further experiments will evaluate the performance of this biomaterial carrier system for controlled therapeutic delivery.

The experiment utilized chemicals acquired from different suppliers. Sigma Aldrich provided the pectin polymer, chloroacetic acid, citric acid, FeCl3·6H2O, and NaOH purchased for the study. Chitosan with a viscosity of 200–600 mPa·s, measured at 0.5 % in 0.5 % acetic acid at 20 °C, and terephthalic acid (TPA) were purchased from TCI in Japan. LC Laboratories, located in Woburn, Massachusetts, USA, supplied Doxorubicin (DOX). Duksan provided N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetic acid, methanol, and ethanol. Only distilled water was used in the study, and it was acquired as well.

The FT-IR spectra were obtained using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (CARY 640, Agilent). Rigaku (Ultima IV) was utilized to capture the X-ray diffraction pattern of the specimens with Cu-Kα radiation from 5° to 80° at a scanning speed of 1°/min. The structure of hydrogels was examined with a MIRA3 TESCAN FE-SEM. EDX analysis was utilized to explore the dimensions of HA/MIL-88/GO within the nanocomposites matrix. The hydrodynamic size and zeta-potential of the materials were assessed using Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) with a Zetasizer Nano-ZS instrument from Malvern, UK, which was fitted with a HeNe laser (633 nm) and an ELS controller set at 90° to control optical data.

The process of making CMCS involved several steps. First, 5 g of chitosan was added to a 20 % w/v NaOH solution and allowed to dissolve for 15 min with stirring. Then, 15 g of chloroacetic acid was gradually introduced to the mixture while maintaining continuous agitation over a period of 2 h at 40 °C. This created a uniform blended solution. To balance the pH, 10 % acetic acid was added next to neutralize the reaction mixture. Following neutralization, the resulting solution was poured into an excess of 70 % methanol. The mixture was filtered using a glass fritted funnel to separate any solids. Multiple washes with methanol helped remove any remaining NaOH or chloroacetic acid. Finally, the filtered CMCS powder obtained was dried in a vacuum oven at 55 °C for 8 h, completing the CMCS production process.

The GQDs were synthesized by pyrolyzing citric acid directly following established procedures. To start, 2 g of citric acid were put into a 5 mL beaker and then heated to 200 °C with a heating mantle. Approximately 30 min later, the gradually added orange liquid was mixed vigorously with 100 mL of a 10 mg/mL NaOH solution. After adding NaOH to neutralize it, a 2 % GQDs solution in water was produced and then refrigerated at 4 °C for future use.

The MIL-88 was synthesized through combining specific reactants and undergoing hydrothermal synthesis. To produce MIL-88, 0.0935 g of FeCl3·6H2O and 0.0575 g of TPA were mixed together in 15 mL of the solvent DMF dimethylformamide. This solution was stirred at room temperature for several minutes to ensure thorough mixing and the production of a homogeneous yellow solution. Next, the mixed solution was sealed inside a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave reactor vessel. The autoclave was then placed inside an oven and heated to 185 °C for a duration of 2 h. This heating process under autogenous pressure, known as hydrothermal synthesis, drove the crystallization of MIL-88 from the reactants. Upon completion of the reaction, a red precipitate had formed. Centrifugation was used to separate this precipitate from the solution. To purify the product, it was repeatedly rinsed with 20 mL each of DMF and ethanol to remove any remaining starting materials or solvent. Finally, the purified MIL-88 crystals were dried inside a vacuum oven.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-situ synthesis of CMCS/GQDs within the Pe/MIL-88 hydrogel beads (CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88)
ce:section.ce:para: The CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 hydrogel beads were prepared in several steps. First, a CMCS solution was made by dissolving 1 g of CMCS in 2 % acetic acid. To incorporate 30 % GQDs, 18 mL of the prepared GQDs dispersion was added to the CMCS solution. This mixture was sonicated to ensure homogeneity. Separately, 1 g of Pe was dissolved in 100 mL of water with stirring. Next, 100 mg of MIL-88 was mixed into the Pe solution while stirring at room temperature. Once uniform solutions were obtained, the CMCS/GQDs mixture was incorporated into the Pe/MIL-88 solution. The combined dispersion was stirred at 25 °C for 48 h to obtain a homogenous mixture. This solution containing CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 was then released as droplets into 200 mL of a cross-linker solution (FeCl3·6H2O, 5 %) using a syringe, from a fixed height of 15 cm above the solution. Spherical hydrogel beads were formed and allowed to solidify via cross-linking for 3 h without stirring. Afterwards, excess cross-linker (Fe+3 ions) was rinsed away from the bead surfaces with distilled water. Finally, the beads were dried in a vacuum oven at 40 °C, yielding the final CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 hydrogel beads.

The swelling capacity and weight loss of the CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 hydrogel beads were examined. The hydrogel beads were placed in solutions with varying pH levels to simulate the environments of the stomach and intestines. First, they were incubated in pH 1.2 buffer for 2 h to represent the stomach's acidic conditions. Next, the beads were transferred to pH 6.8 buffer for 2 more hours to mimic the small intestine. Finally, the swelling was assessed in pH 7.4 buffer over 4 h to replicate conditions in the large intestine and blood. During each time period, samples of the beads were removed from the buffer at regular intervals. Excess liquid was blotted away, and the weights of the swollen hydrogels were recorded. The swelling percentage was calculated using the Eqs. (1) and (2) that divides the difference between the swollen (Ws) and dry (Wd) weights by the dry weight. This allowed the absorption capacities of the hydrogels under different pH environments to be systematically tested and quantified.
(1)
Swelling ratio
%
=
W s
−
W d
W d
× 100
(2)
Weight loss
%
=
W d
−
W s
W d
× 100

The process of incorporating DOX into the CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 hydrogel commenced by immersing 100 mg samples of the dry composite in 10 mL of a 100 ppm DOX solution. The samples were stirred constantly in darkness for 72 h to facilitate the diffusion and absorption of DOX into the porous hydrogel matrix. After this loading phase, the leftover solution was examined using UV–vis spectroscopy to assess the concentration of unabsorbed DOX that remained in the supernatant. This enabled the calculation of loading capacity by measuring the change between initial and final DOX quantities. The drug-loaded hydrogel samples were subsequently gathered and completely washed with distilled water to eliminate any DOX that was not embedded in the material. Following the wash, the samples were air-dried at room temperature to prepare for characterization and evaluation of drug release properties.

In-vitro drug release experiments were conducted to emulate conditions of the gastrointestinal tract, with certain modifications relative to earlier research [35]. The beads that were created were used to evaluate the release of the drug in vitro by being successively immersed in 10 mL buffered solutions with pH values of 1.2 (for 2 h), 6.8 (for 2 h), and 7.4 (for 4 h). Samples were gathered at regular intervals, and an equal volume of fresh buffer was added each time to maintain a consistent buffer volume. The standard DOX calibration curve at a wavelength of 483 nm was employed to assess both the DOX loading capacity and the overall release profile. Eq. 3 was employed to determine the loading capacity, while Eq. 4 was utilized to assess the cumulative release profile.
(3)
Drug loading
%
=
The amount of drug
g
The amount of nanohybrid and drug
g
× 100
(4)
Drug release
%
=
The amount of released drug
The amount of
l oaded drug
× 100

The prepared samples were tested for cell toxicity using the MTT assay in an in-vitro setting. In line with the standard protocol, K562 cells (cell lines of human blood cancer) were chosen. In short, K562 cells were plated in 96-well dishes with 5000 cells per well in 100 μL of cell medium and left to incubate overnight. Next, the mixture underwent treatment with DOX alone, DOX combined with CMCS/Pe, and DOX combined with CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88, all having the same amount of DOX (32, 16, 8, 4, and 2 μg/mL). Furthermore, to assess biocompatibility, the cells were exposed to empty nano-carrier. Cell toxicity was evaluated following a 72 h incubation period. Following the removal of the culture medium from each well, 20 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was applied to each well. After incubating at 37 °C for 4 h, the culture medium was taken out and 200 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was introduced to dissolve the formazan crystals. The cell count was determined through manual counting using a hemacytometer. Culturing experiments were conducted three times for each type of sample. A Zeiss inverted microscope was utilized to assess the cell morphology. The Zeiss Axoscope 2 Plus fluorescence microscope was used with the required DNA filter sets for staining with 1 mg/mL Hoechst 33,258 in PBS for 5 min. After that, it was washed twice with PBS for 5 min before being examined.

The schematic illustration in Scheme 1 demonstrates the stepwise synthesis of the CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel beads. MIL-88, an iron-based MOF, was first stirred with pectin to form the Pe/MIL-88 complex. Pe, known for its biocompatibility and gelling properties, interacts with MIL-88 through hydrogen bonding between the COOH groups of Pe and the OH groups of MIL-88. Separately, CMCS was stirred with GQDs. CMCS provides a flexible and biocompatible polymeric framework. The GQDs were functionalized with OH groups, which interact with the hydroxyl and amine groups of CMCS, forming the CMCS/GQDs composite. The interaction between CMCS and GQDs is also stabilized through hydrogen bonding, as indicated in the schematic. After forming the CMCS/GQDs and Pe/MIL-88 composites, they were combined by stirring for 48 h. The resulting CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 structure is a hybrid nanocomposite where the Pe/MIL-88 complex is uniformly distributed within the CMCS/GQDs polymeric structure. The hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl groups of all components (CMCS, GQDs, Pe, and MIL-88) is crucial for forming a stable and integrated nanocomposite. The final step involved the conversion of the CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 hybrid into hydrogel beads. This was achieved by dripping the composite solution into an FeCl3 solution, inducing cross-linking and bead formation. The Fe3+ ions from FeCl₃ act as cross-linking agents, promoting the formation of a three-dimensional network structure that results in CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel beads, as depicted in the lower section of Scheme 1. The resulting hydrogel beads are shown to be uniform and spherical, with a stable morphology suitable for various applications, such as drug delivery or environmental remediation [6,24].

FT-IR spectroscopy was employed for the examination of the chemical composition of CMCS, Pe, MIL-88, DOX, CMCS/Pe hydrogel beads, CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel beads, and CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88/DOX nanocomposite hydrogel beads. The DOX spectra showed various bands related to different molecular vibrations: OH stretching at 3526 cm−1 and 3322 cm−1, CH stretching at 2902 cm−1, CO stretching at 1732 cm−1, NH bending at 1588 cm−1, aromatic ring vibrations at 1411 cm−1 and 1290 cm−1, and CH out-of-plane vibration at 792 cm−1, indicating the existence of DOX. The MIL-88 spectra showed specific peaks for COO– at 744 cm−1, CO at 1387 cm−1, and CO at 1543 cm−1 related to the stretching modes of the TPA ligands, along with a peak possibly linked to hydroxyl groups or moisture [25,36]. The CMCS spectra showed peaks related to OH stretching at 3346 cm−1 and asymmetric and symmetric stretching of COO− at 1671 cm−1 and 1480 cm−1. The spectra from Pe displayed bands that represented the stretching vibrations of –CH and intramolecular hydrogen bonds of –OH groups, as well as the esterified carboxyl groups, the free carboxyl group, the bending vibrations of O and CO, and the CH bending vibration at 3309 and 2901 cm−1, 1746 cm−1, 1613 cm−1, 1307 and 1038 cm−1, and 723 cm−1. The individual components in the spectra of CMCS/Pe hydrogel beads, CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel beads, and CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88/DOX nanocomposite hydrogel beads were confirmed, indicating the successful preparation and integration of these materials. The CMCS/Pe hybrid displayed combined peaks from CMCS and Pe, with some shifts due to Van der Waals interactions. The CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel beads showed integration of groups from CMCS, Pe, GQD, and MIL-88 confirming successful synthesis. The CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88/DOX hydrogel spectra exhibited a broad peak at 3307 cm−1 for OH stretching, slightly higher than other samples. The 1734 cm−1 peak, attributed to CO stretching, may be influenced by DOX. The 1244 cm−1 peak indicates CO stretching from MIL-88 or DOX. DOX incorporation slightly shifts existing peaks, suggesting integration into the matrix without forming new functional groups. These interactions are crucial for drug delivery applications, ensuring controlled release and therapeutic efficacy (Fig. 1 ).

The XRD patterns of CMCS, Pe, MIL-88, and the CMCS/Pe hydrogel beads and the CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel beads were examined in the 2θ range of 5–80°. In Fig. 2a and b, pure CMCS showed peaks at 9.4° and 19.9° while Pe exhibited a wide peak at 21° 2θ, corresponding to its short-range ordered structure [37,38]. The MIL-88 XRD showed main diffraction peaks at 9.4°, 12.5°, 16.6° and 19.2° 2θ, consistent with previous reports (Fig. 2c) [39]. The CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite pattern showed a broad peak around 22.1° 2θ, denoting the amorphous nature of the CMCS/Pe component. It also resembled the MIL-88 pattern, suggesting the preserved MIL-88 structure. Peaks around 10.8°, 17.3°, 25.6° and 27.9° 2θ further verified the inclusion of MIL-88 within the CMCS/Pe network. No extra peaks were detected, validating the incorporation of MIL-88 into the polymer matrix. In summary, XRD analysis identified the crystalline components in each material and confirmed the production of MIL-88 and its embedding within the synthetic materials (Fig. 2e) [40,41]. A wide peak at 20° is due to the pure CMCS/Pe hydrogel beads without the presence of MIL-88 that shown in Fig. 2d.

The graph shows an adsorption (ADS)-desorption (DES) isotherm plot that is utilized to quantify the surface area of porous substances. It does this by gauging the quantity of nitrogen gas absorbed and released at varying levels of partial pressure. This specific isotherm follows a Type IV curve configuration. Materials that demonstrate this pattern typically possess pores within a particular size range, from 2 to 50 nm wide, qualifying them as falling under the category of mesoporous materials. The Type IV isotherm classification is a signature indicator that can help identify samples containing this class of textural porosity when examining their adsorption behaviors. The initial region (approximately p/p0 < 0.2) shows a relatively flat increase in adsorption volume, indicating monolayer adsorption. This is typical for BET analysis at lower pressures where gas molecules begin to cover the surface of the material. As the relative pressure increases (between p/p0 = 0.4 and p/p0 = 0.8), the adsorption volume rises more steeply, indicating the formation of multilayers of adsorbed gas as well as capillary condensation within the pores. The steep increase near p/p0 ∼ 0.9 suggests the presence of mesopores, where capillary condensation occurs. The gap between the ADS and DES curves forms a hysteresis loop, which is indicative of mesoporous materials. This hysteresis loop is most prominent at higher relative pressures (p/p0 > 0.6), reflecting capillary condensation and pore filling/emptying (Fig. 3 ). The hysteresis loop is also associated with pore size distribution and shape. The presence of this loop suggests that the CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel beads contain pores that facilitate gas trapping during adsorption, which are gradually released during desorption. The pores provide not only a large surface area for drug adsorption but also a controlled release pathway due to the pore structure and capillary condensation effects. The pore size distribution is important for the release kinetics of drugs, particularly in biomedical applications [35,41].

The zeta potential results provided valuable insights into the surface charge properties of the synthesized nanomaterials. The zeta potential of the GQDs is shown to be approximately −18 mV. This negative value indicates that the GQDs possess a substantial negative surface charge, which is expected due to the presence of functional groups on the GQDs that contribute to this charge. A high negative zeta potential generally suggests good colloidal stability due to electrostatic repulsion between particles, minimizing the likelihood of aggregation in solution. The zeta potential of the CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel beads is around −8 mV. This less negative zeta potential compared to the GQDs alone suggests that the incorporation of CMCS, Pe, and MIL-88 into the composite alters the surface charge. The reduction in negative charge may be due to the interaction between the GQDs and other components, such as CMCS and MIL-88, which could neutralize some of the surface charges of the GQDs. A zeta potential around −8 mV indicates moderate stability, meaning the particles in the composite might have some tendency to aggregate, particularly in comparison to the more stable GQDs (Fig. 4a) [42].

The hydrodynamic size of the CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel beads was determined using DLS measurement in an aqueous solution as shown in Fig. 4b. The graph shows two distinct peaks, indicating the presence of two main particle size populations within the nanocomposite. The first and most prominent peak is centered around 250–300 nm, with a maximum intensity reaching 62 %. This suggests that the majority of particles in the composite have a size within this range. The second, smaller peak occurs around 400–450 nm, with a maximum intensity of 28 %. This indicates the presence of a smaller population of relatively larger particles in the composite. The presence of multiple peaks suggests that the CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel beads have a heterogeneous size distribution. The nanocomposite exhibited a wider particle size distribution that could have resulted from either the clustering of smaller particles or the formation of rod-shaped MIL-88 structures, as noted previously. The spread of particle sizes has important implications for the material's performance in biomedical uses like drug delivery. Specifically, smaller sizes may enhance a carrier's ability to cross biological membranes, an advantage for delivering drugs. Meanwhile, larger particles could transport higher amounts of medication due to their increased capacity to hold drug payloads. In summary, the particle size distribution determines how effective the nanocomposite might be when applied to therapies that rely on its interaction with living systems on a nanoscale level. The DLS results confirm that the CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite beads have a suitable particle size distribution for potential applications in targeted drug delivery, with the majority of particles in a size range that is optimal for such applications [17].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX)
ce:section.ce:para: The surface characteristics of a drug carrier play a crucial role in determining its release behavior. To examine the surface morphology of the samples, FE-SEM was employed. The Fig. 5a exhibit the MIL-88 particles is a rod-like morphology with clear and distinct elongated structures. The rods appear to be well-dispersed and uniformly distributed, with a relatively smooth surface. This characteristic rod-like structure is a typical feature of MIL-88, which is composed of metal ions (such as Fe) and organic linkers (such as TPA) [43]. The Fig. 5b showed the surface morphology of the CMCS/Pe hydrogel beads. The surface appears smooth with some fibrous structures, indicating the presence of CMCS and Pe. The image suggests a homogenous and compact structure, with some visible network formation typical of cross-linked polymeric hydrogels. The Fig. 5c provides a closer look at the surface of the CMCS/GQD@Pe/MIL-88 hydrogel beads. The image shows that the surface is populated with small, granular structures, which likely represent the GQDs and MIL-88 particles embedded within the hydrogel matrix. The presence of these nanoparticles on the surface suggests successful incorporation and dispersion of GQDs within the hydrogel beads. The rough and textured appearance of the surface indicates the presence of these nano-scale materials, contributing to the overall functionality of the hydrogel for drug delivery and other applications.

Elemental analysis of the CMCS/GQD@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite beads was conducted using EDX (shown in Fig. 5d). The EDX spectrum verified the high purity of the synthesized CMCS/GQD@Pe/MIL-88 material, only detecting C, N, O, and Fe. The approximate weight percentages of these elements were 58.27 % for C, 3.12 % for N, 28.63 % for O, and 9.97 % for Fe. This detection of these specific elements in the provided percentages provides evidence that the intended CMCS/GQD@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite beads was successfully formed without impurities within the detectable limits of the EDX analysis.

Understanding how the CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel beads swell, as shown in Fig. 6a is important for determining how the hydrogel behaves under different pH conditions that simulate the gastrointestinal tract. The degree of swelling provides insight into the hydrogel's water absorption and expansion capabilities, which are critical for applications like drug delivery where the rate of drug release can be affected by the hydrogel's swelling properties. As depicted, both types of hydrogel beads exhibit little swelling in acidic conditions resembling the stomach's pH of 1.2. Their minimal swelling in this environment suggests they would likely retain any drug payload while in the stomach and start releasing it once transiting to less acidic areas of the gastrointestinal tract, making them suitable for oral drug delivery where controlled release is desired. The swelling percentage remains relatively low, with slight increases over the 2 h period. This low swelling can be attributed to the acidic conditions, where the ionization of functional groups in the polymers is suppressed, leading to a more compact network structure that resists water uptake [44]. Upon transitioning to pH 6.8, which simulates the environment of the small intestine, a noticeable increase in swelling is observed for both hydrogels. The CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel beads show a sharper increase in swelling compared to the CMCS/Pe beads. This enhanced swelling in the nanocomposite can be attributed to the presence of GQDs and MIL-88, which might contribute to an increased network elasticity and porosity, allowing more water to penetrate and expand the hydrogel structure. The most significant swelling occurs in the pH 7.4 environment, which mimics the conditions of the large intestine and blood. The CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel beads reach a higher swelling percentage than the CMCS/Pe hydrogel beads, indicating that the nanocomposite has a greater capacity to absorb water under neutral to slightly basic conditions. This increased swelling can be linked to the ionization of carboxyl and other functional groups within the hydrogel matrix, leading to electrostatic repulsion that expands the network and allows more water to be absorbed. The enhanced swelling in the nanocomposite could be due to the increased surface area provided by the MIL-88 and GQDs, which allows for more water interaction and absorption. Additionally, the presence of GQDs might contribute to the stability and elasticity of the hydrogel network, enabling it to accommodate more water as the pH increases [45,46].

The weight loss analysis depicted in the Fig. 6b provides crucial insights into the degradation behavior of CMCS/Pe and CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 hydrogel beads over a period of 8 h. This analysis is essential for understanding the stability and potential utility of these hydrogels, especially in applications like drug delivery, where controlled degradation and release over time are desirable. In initial Weight Loss (2 h), the red bars show that the CMCS/Pe hydrogel exhibits a 35 % weight loss after 2 h. On the other hand, the CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 hydrogel beads (blue bars) show a higher weight loss of 48 % at the same time point. The incorporation of GQDs and MIL-88 into the hydrogel matrix seems to accelerate the initial weight loss, likely due to the increased porosity and surface area provided by the GQDs and MIL-88, which enhances water absorption and network swelling, leading to faster degradation. After 4 h, the weight loss of hydrogel increases to 48 % and the weight loss of nanocomposite hydrogel jumps to 69 %. After 6 h, the weight loss of hydrogel reaches 92 % and the weight loss for the nanocomposite hydrogel is slightly lower at 83 %. The CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 hydrogel shows a more consistent and gradual degradation compared to CMCS/Pe, which undergoes a faster degradation after 6 h. This difference may be attributed to the interaction between CMCS, Pe, GQDs, and MIL-88. The GQDs and MIL-88 help stabilize the hydrogel matrix, slowing down degradation as the material becomes more saturated with water. In final weight loss the CMCS/Pe hydrogel beads show complete degradation, reaching 100 % weight loss. The controlled degradation profile exhibited by the CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 hydrogel beads, especially their greater resistance to complete breakdown compared to CMCS/Pe, underscores their potential as drug delivery vehicles. The slower degradation would allow for a sustained release of drugs over a longer period, enhancing therapeutic efficacy [47,48].

Fig. 7a and b shows the normal calibration curve and total release patterns of DOX from CMCS/Pe hydrogel beads and CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 hydrogel beads at three distinct pH levels: 1.2 (mimicking the stomach's acidity), 6.8 (mimicking the small intestine), and 7.4 (representing the large intestine and blood flow). The absorbance values of different concentrations of DOX (in μg/mL) were plotted against their corresponding absorbance values to create the standard calibration curve shown in Fig. 7a. The strong correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9993) validates the precision of the calibration curve, establishing it as a trustworthy method for measuring the unidentified concentrations of DOX in the drug-loading process. During the initial release phase (0−2 h), both hydrogels exhibit minimal drug release at the low pH, indicating that DOX is effectively retained in the acidic stomach environment. Although CMCS/Pe hydrogel releases slightly more DOX than CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88, both hydrogels maintain a gradual release profile. In the intermediate release phase (2–4 h), a significant increase in DOX release is observed at pH 6.8 for both hydrogels. CMCS/Pe continues to release more drug compared to CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88, though the difference remains relatively small. In the final release phase (4–8 h), the release rate increases markedly at pH 7.4, with CMCS/Pe hydrogel showing a faster and higher cumulative release than CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 (Fig. 7). This difference could be attributed to the pH-sensitive properties of the CMCS polymer and the diffusion barrier created by the beads [49]. CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88, however, demonstrates a more controlled and sustained release over time, which is advantageous for prolonged drug delivery. While CMCS/Pe hydrogel provides a faster release rate, making it suitable for applications requiring rapid drug release, CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 offers a slower, more controlled release, ideal for prolonged or targeted drug delivery, particularly in the bloodstream or tumor microenvironments [16].

The cytotoxicity of CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 was assessed using the MTT assay, with a free blank solution serving as a control. As shown in Fig. 8 , free DOX, DOX-loaded CMCS/Pe, and DOX-loaded CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 all exhibited dose-dependent cytotoxicity against K562 cells, with higher drug concentrations correlating with increased cytotoxicity. At a concentration of 2 μM DOX, cell viability decreased significantly. Notably, CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88/DOX displayed the lowest cell viability (∼67 %), suggesting it exerted a stronger cytotoxic effect compared to free DOX and CMCS/Pe/DOX, where viability was higher (∼50–80 %). This trend persisted at 4 μM and 8 μM, with CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88/DOX demonstrating greater cytotoxicity than the other formulations. Free DOX consistently showed the least cytotoxicity at these concentrations, indicating the nanoparticles effectively enhance the drug's potency. At 16 μM, CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88/DOX continued to show the most significant reduction in cell viability (∼28 %), while free DOX resulted in approximately 25 % viability, and CMCS/Pe/DOX showed about 45 % viability. At 32 μM, all formulations exhibited similar cytotoxicity, with cell viability dropping below 30 % for all samples. These findings demonstrate that CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88/DOX consistently achieves the highest cytotoxic effect across all DOX concentrations tested. This indicates that the nanocomposite hydrogel beads significantly enhance the efficacy of DOX in killing cancer cells. The combination of GQDs, MIL-88, and CMCS likely improves DOX delivery and cellular uptake, resulting in more efficient drug release and therapeutic action [17,35].

This research developed a new nanocomposite hydrogel bead for targeted drug delivery applications. Specifically, we synthesized a carboxymethyl chitosan/graphene quantum dots encapsulated within a pectin and MIL-88 matrix (CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88) that could encapsulate and release the chemotherapy drug doxorubicin in a controlled manner. Using a simple, green one-step process, we integrated the CMCS, GQDs, Pe and MIL-88 framework materials into a hydrogel network structure. Characterization methods such as FT-IR, XRD, BET, DLS, and FE-SEM verified the successful formation of the nanocomposite system and offered understanding of its composition and surface properties on a nanoscale level. This new drug transporter shows potential in enhancing cancer therapies by aiding in the transport of chemotherapy drugs to tumors. The composite exhibited favorable swelling behaviors under varying pH conditions, which is crucial for applications in oral drug delivery systems that require pH-sensitive release profiles. The CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 beads demonstrated enhanced swelling at neutral to slightly basic pH levels, particularly at pH 7.4, indicating their suitability for drug release in the intestinal and bloodstream environments. Additionally, the incorporation of GQDs and MIL-88 into the hydrogel matrix improved both the drug loading capacity and the release kinetics of DOX, highlighting the composite's potential for targeted cancer therapy. The cytotoxicity studies using K562 cancer cells further validated the biocompatibility of the nanocomposite, showing that the DOX-loaded hydrogel beads could effectively reduce cell viability while maintaining biocompatibility with non-cancerous cells. The controlled degradation and high drug release potential of the CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 system suggest that it could serve as a promising platform for controlled drug delivery, particularly in cancer treatment applications. Future work will focus on in vivo studies to further assess the therapeutic efficacy and biocompatibility of the CMCS/GQDs@Pe/MIL-88 nanocomposite hydrogel beads. Additionally, expanding the range of drugs encapsulated within this carrier system will provide valuable insights into its versatility and broader biomedical applications.

Iman Gholamali: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mehdi Yadollahi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.
Not applicable.
Not applicable.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.
